title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Amartya Sanyal is an Assistant Professor of Machine Learning at University of Copenhagen whose research focuses on trustworthy machine learning, particularly privacy, robustness, and fairness under limited or adversarial conditions. His recent work spans differential privacy in online learning and LLMs, machine unlearning mechanisms, and understanding vulnerabilities in deep learning models to noisy data and distribution shifts.

name: Ankit Kariryaa
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Ankit Kariryaa is an Assistant Professor working on machine learning applications for earth observation, with a focus on using satellite and aerial imagery combined with deep learning to understand and map vegetation at scale—particularly individual trees and forest ecosystems in drylands and across different biomes globally.

name: Bernhard Kerbl
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Bernhard Kerbl is a computer graphics researcher specializing in real-time rendering techniques, particularly 3D Gaussian Splatting and radiance field methods for novel-view synthesis. His work focuses on efficient GPU-based algorithms for visualizing and interacting with massive 3D datasets in real-time.

name: Boel Nelson
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Boel Nelson is a tenure-track assistant professor focusing on privacy in computer systems, spanning from data collection to publication. Her research addresses how data can be protected as it flows through systems, with expertise in differential privacy, side-channel detection, and real-world deployment of privacy-preserving techniques.

name: Hang Yin
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Hang Yin is an assistant professor working on learning-based robotics, integrating task representation, dynamical systems, and optimization-based control to enable robots to efficiently learn manipulation and motor skills from data in physical environments.

name: Jacob Holm
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Jacob Holm is a tenure-track assistant professor in the Algorithms and Complexity group at DIKU, University of Copenhagen. He specializes in developing efficient algorithms for dynamic graph problems, particularly planarity testing, connectivity, and structural properties of planar graphs, with contributions to fundamental data structures like top trees and biconnectivity maintenance.

name: Julius Bier Kirkegaard
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Julius Bier Kirkegaard conducts theoretical and data-centric research spanning biological physics, computational modeling, and image analysis. His work focuses on optimal strategies in biological systems (chemotaxis, morphogenesis), protein dynamics, and developing computational methods like deep learning for microscopy and differentiable programming.

name: Nirupam Gupta
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Nirupam Gupta works on distributed and federated machine learning algorithms, with a focus on ensuring robustness, fairness, and privacy against Byzantine attacks, data poisoning, and adversarial behaviors in distributed settings.

name: Pepa Kostadinova Atanasova
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Pepa Kostadinova Atanasova is an Assistant Professor in Natural Language Processing at University of Copenhagen focusing on explainability and accountability of machine learning models. Her research addresses interpretability of large language models, robustness of explanation methods, factuality maintenance, and complex reasoning tasks like fact-checking and question answering.

name: Raghavendra Selvan
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Raghavendra Selvan is an Assistant Professor at the University of Copenhagen whose research spans sustainable machine learning, machine learning for sciences, medical image analysis, and graph neural networks, with a particular focus on developing resource-efficient and environmentally conscious AI methods.

name: Sadegh Talebi
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Sadegh Talebi is an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at the University of Copenhagen specializing in the theoretical foundations of reinforcement learning, including online learning, multi-armed bandits, Markov decision processes, and resource allocation in networks. His research focuses on algorithmic aspects of sequential decision-making, regret minimization, and learning in both Markovian and non-Markovian environments.

name: Sarah Frances Homewood
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Sarah Homewood researches how the design of self-tracking technologies reflects and shapes societal perspectives on bodies, with particular focus on menstrual tracking, fertility apps, and chronic illnesses like post-COVID syndrome. Her work combines qualitative research, design prototyping, and long-term user testing to understand current technology use and develop alternative designs that propose new bodily perspectives.

name: Teresa Hirzle
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Teresa Hirzle is a tenure-track assistant professor at the University of Copenhagen whose research focuses on Extended Reality (XR) for behavior change and the intersection of XR and artificial intelligence. Her work spans human-computer interaction design, eye tracking systems, gaze-based interaction on head-mounted displays, and applications of immersive technologies for health and well-being.

name: Troels Henriksen
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Troels Henriksen is an assistant professor working on optimizing compilers for data-parallel functional languages, with a focus on the Futhark language which compiles functional array programs to efficient GPU code while maintaining high-level functional abstractions.

name: Valkyrie Savage
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Valkyrie Savage researches human-computer interaction with a focus on input devices, sensing techniques, and digital fabrication. Her work creates functional interactive prototypes by integrating sensing (acoustic, capacitive, vision-based) into 3D-printed objects to enable deeply customized interfaces adapted to users' bodies and needs.

name: Jeppe Have Rasmussen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Jeppe Have Rasmussen studies animal communication and sound production across birds, whales, and fish using bioacoustics, biomechanics, and deep learning methods. His recent work focuses on using acoustic monitoring and artificial intelligence for biodiversity assessment in marine and terrestrial ecosystems.

name: Ilias Chalkidis
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Ilias Chalkidis is an Assistant Professor working on natural language processing applications to legal and political texts, with focus on legal NLP tasks including judgment prediction and multi-label text classification, as well as recent work on applying large language models to analyze political discourse and populism.

name: Martin Nørgaard
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Martin Nørgaard is a biomedical engineer and neuroscientist who studies how methodological variability affects neuroimaging data interpretation, with focus on optimizing preprocessing pipelines in PET/MR imaging and improving reliability and reproducibility in medical image analyses through machine learning and open science practices.

name: Michael Kirkedal Thomsen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Michael Kirkedal Thomsen is an Associate Professor researching reversible computation, including design of reversible programming languages, logic circuits, and systems that enable bidirectional execution while minimizing energy consumption through program transformation techniques.

name: Mostafa Mehdipour Ghazi
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Mostafa Mehdipour Ghazi is an Assistant Professor working on deep learning and machine learning applications to medical image analysis, with specific focus on disease progression modeling, neuroimaging biomarkers, and automated segmentation of anatomical structures in cardiac and brain MRI.

name: Stefan Oehmcke
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Stefan Oehmcke is an Assistant Professor at DIKU working on machine learning applications to geospatial and environmental problems, particularly using deep learning with satellite imagery, LiDAR, and UAV data for forest biomass prediction, crop monitoring, and environmental assessment. His research combines computer vision and temporal-spatial data analysis for large-scale ecological monitoring and climate-related applications.

name: Svetlana Kutuzova
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Svetlana Kutuzova develops deep learning and representation learning methods for analyzing complex biological data, particularly focusing on metagenomic binning, microbiome characterization, and plant-microbe interactions using multimodal generative models and autoencoders.

name: Vikrant Singhal
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Vikrant Singhal works on differential privacy and the intersection of privacy, statistics, and machine learning, focusing on developing computationally-efficient algorithms for private learning and estimation of distributions.

name: Marek Mutwil
title: Associate Professor
summary: Marek Mutwil combines bioinformatics, machine learning, data science, and experimental biology to study the evolution of the plant kingdom from the perspective of gene expression. He uses computational biology to study gene co-function networks to understand how genes work together to express plant traits and to elucidate how plants evolve new pathways.

name: Martin Rainer Bladt
title: Associate Professor
summary: Martin Bladt is an associate professor at the University of Copenhagen working in statistical theory, probability, and actuarial mathematics. His research focuses on developing statistical methods for heavy-tailed and extreme value problems, particularly in insurance applications, including phase-type distributions, extreme value estimation under censoring, claims reserving, and Markov process modeling.

name: Mogens Bladt
title: Professor
summary: Mogens Bladt is a Professor of Applied Probability and Insurance Mathematics who develops matrix-based methods for stochastic processes, with applications in insurance, finance, and operations research. His research focuses on phase-type distributions, matrix-exponential representations, diffusion bridge simulation, and statistical inference for complex probability models.

name: Trine Krogh Boomsma
title: Professor
summary: Boomsma applies stochastic programming, real options analysis, and optimization methods to problems in renewable energy markets and power systems, including investment timing decisions, electricity market bidding, and dynamic fleet management under uncertainty.

name: Robert Wayne Burklund
title: Associate Professor
summary: Robert Burklund works in higher algebra, focusing on computational frameworks for homotopy theory, where geometric objects are studied up to continuous deformation. His thesis established highly coherent multiplications on certain fundamental spaces and provided classification results for highly connected high dimensional manifolds, answering longstanding questions.

name: Matthias Christandl
title: Professor
summary: Matthias Christandl is a professor of mathematical physics at the University of Copenhagen who works on understanding how quantum mechanics impacts information processing. His research spans quantum information theory, tensor theory, and algebraic complexity—with fundamental results on tensors and their applications to condensed matter physics via tensor networks, quantum entanglement, quantum communication, and the computational complexity of matrix multiplication.

name: Line Katrine Harder Clemmensen
title: Professor
summary: Line Clemmensen is a professor at the University of Copenhagen who specializes in statistical modelling and machine learning with emphasis on interpretability and explainability. Her research focuses on developing trustworthy, fair, and explainable models for low-resource domains and high-dimensional data, with applications spanning biotechnology, speech processing, mental health assessment, and environmental science.

name: Jeffrey F. Collamore
title: Professor
summary: Collamore is a probability theorist who develops large deviations methods and asymptotic techniques for studying extreme events in stochastic systems, with applications to insurance ruin problems, financial time series modeling, and quantitative risk management. His work focuses on tail distributions of random recursive equations, rare event probabilities, and multidimensional risk models driven by random matrices.

name: Susanne Ditlevsen
title: Professor
summary: Susanne Ditlevsen is a Professor of Statistics and Stochastic Models in Biology at University of Copenhagen who develops mathematical and statistical methods for modeling physiological systems, neural dynamics, and biological processes using stochastic differential equations, with recent applications to climate tipping points and animal behavior.

name: Søren Eilers
title: Professor
summary: Søren Eilers is a mathematician specializing in operator algebras, particularly the classification of C*-algebras associated with discrete and low-dimensional structures, with emphasis on K-theory invariants and symbolic dynamics. He also leads research on experimental (computer-based) methods in pure mathematics, particularly in number theory and operator algebras.

name: Hélène Esnault
title: Professor
summary: Hélène Esnault is a leading mathematician specializing in algebraic and arithmetic geometry, with particular focus on local systems, fundamental groups, and the connections between complex and arithmetic geometry. She has proven major conjectures including the Lang-Manin conjecture over finite fields and properties of crystalline rigid local systems.

name: Elisenda Feliu
title: Professor
summary: Elisenda Feliu is a Professor and head of the Applied Algebra research group at University of Copenhagen, working on applied algebraic geometry methods to understand biochemical reaction networks. Her research focuses on determining when reaction networks display multiple steady states, bistability, and oscillatory behavior using algebraic and computational approaches.

name: Søren Fournais
title: Professor
summary: Søren Fournais is a leading mathematical physicist who studies the mathematical foundations of quantum mechanics, particularly focusing on understanding Bose-Einstein condensates and their phase transitions. His work involves proving rigorous mathematical solutions to quantum mechanical equations and analyzing spectral properties of quantum systems with applications to condensed matter physics.

name: Jesper Grodal
title: Professor
summary: Jesper Grodal is a professor of mathematics at the University of Copenhagen specializing in algebraic topology, group theory, and their interactions. His research focuses on homotopical group theory, the classification of p-compact groups, and applications to finite loop spaces and group cohomology.

name: Ernst Hansen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Ernst Hansen is a mathematician and statistician whose research spans dynamical systems theory, stochastic processes, and applied statistics. His work includes bifurcation theory, continuous-time Markov chain methods for financial and epidemiological applications, and mathematical foundations for probability and statistics.

name: Niels Richard Hansen
title: Professor
summary: Niels Richard Hansen is a professor of computational statistics at the University of Copenhagen who develops statistical methods and models for data analysis, prediction, and classification at the intersection of AI and statistics. His research focuses on causal inference, conditional independence testing, learning dynamical systems from data, and sparse statistical methods for high-dimensional problems.

name: Lars Hesselholt
title: Professor
summary: Lars Hesselholt is a mathematician specializing in homotopy theory, algebraic K-theory, and arithmetic algebraic geometry. His recent work includes developing Dirac geometry and investigating connections between K-theory, topological cyclic homology, and arithmetic invariants in commutative algebra.

name: Munir Eberhardt Hiabu
title: Associate Professor
summary: Munir Hiabu is an Associate Professor of Actuarial Mathematics and Data Science at University of Copenhagen, working on nonparametric statistics and statistical machine learning methods for insurance applications, biostatistics, and mortality analysis, with particular emphasis on interpretability and understanding how high-dimensional data structure can enhance predictions and risk quantification.

name: Henrik Granau Holm
title: Professor
summary: Henrik Holm is a professor of mathematics specializing in homological algebra, ring theory, and category theory, with particular expertise in Gorenstein homological algebra and derived category methods in commutative algebra. He has published over 30 research papers and co-authored a graduate textbook on derived category methods.

name: Ryomei Iwasa
title: Associate Professor
summary: Ryomei Iwasa is a mathematician specializing in motivic stable homotopy theory and algebraic K-theory. His research develops foundational frameworks for cohomology theories in algebraic and analytic geometry, particularly extending motivic stable homotopy theory beyond classical A¹-homotopy invariance to connect with p-adic and complex geometry.

name: Ian Kiming
title: Professor
summary: Ian Kiming is a professor at University of Copenhagen who specializes in algebraic number theory, particularly studying the arithmetic properties of modular forms and their connections to Galois representations. His research focuses on p-adic methods, modular forms modulo prime powers, and the computational and theoretical aspects of these structures.

name: Tinne Hoff Kjeldsen
title: Professor
summary: Tinne Hoff Kjeldsen is a professor at the University of Copenhagen who specializes in the history and philosophy of modern mathematics, with particular focus on 20th-century developments in operations research, nonlinear programming, and convexity. Her research also encompasses mathematics education, mathematical modelling in scientific contexts, and the pedagogical integration of historical perspectives into mathematics teaching.

name: Joachim Kock
title: Associate Professor
summary: Joachim Kock works primarily in category theory, homotopy theory, and algebraic geometry with applications to combinatorics, mathematical physics, and theoretical computer science. His recent work focuses on Petri nets and higher-categorical structures, including research on Quillen's conjecture regarding finite group homotopy theory.

name: Jesper Lützen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Jesper Lützen is a historian of mathematics and physical sciences who studies the development of mathematical concepts including distribution theory, differential equations, impossibility results, and 19th-century mechanics through figures like Joseph Liouville and Heinrich Hertz.

name: Laura Mancinska
title: Associate Professor
summary: Laura Mancinska is a theoretician studying quantum computing who focuses on understanding quantum entanglement, nonlocal games, and their properties from mathematical and complexity-theoretic perspectives. Her work aims to identify quantum advantages over classical information processing and develop tools for verifying quantum technologies.

name: Bo Markussen
title: Professor
summary: Bo Markussen is a Professor of Statistics at the University of Copenhagen whose main theoretical research areas are functional data analysis (FDA) and multiple testing. He develops operator-based methods for multivariate functional data and new methods for adjusting p-values that account for correlations between tests, while also leading the Data Science Lab's collaborative research across theoretical statistics and applied projects.

name: Jasmin Matz
title: Associate Professor
summary: Jasmin Matz works in number theory and representation theory, focusing on the Arthur trace formula and its applications to understanding automorphic representations and spectral properties of arithmetic groups acting on symmetric spaces. Her research combines analytic and algebraic methods to study asymptotic behavior of eigenvalues and geometric invariants of arithmetic quotients.

name: Marek Leonard Miller
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Marek Miller studies mathematical aspects of quantum information theory, focusing on positive maps of operator algebras, CP-divisibility, quantum dynamical semigroups, quantum resource theory, and quantum catalysis. His recent work includes large-scale simulation of quantum systems using Trotter product formulas for Hamiltonian time evolution.

name: Niels Martin Møller
title: Associate Professor
summary: Niels Martin Møller works on geometric analysis, where differential geometry and topology intersect with nonlinear partial differential equations. His research focuses on mean curvature flows, minimal surfaces, self-shrinkers, and translating solitons, with applications to understanding geometric singularities and topological problems.

name: Magdalena Elena Musat
title: Professor
summary: Magdalena Musat is an expert in functional analytical aspects of operator algebras with research spanning operator space theory, von Neumann algebras, completely positive maps, and their applications to quantum information theory, including infinite-dimensional phenomena in quantum channels and factorizable maps.

name: Asbjørn Christian Nordentoft
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Nordentoft is an analytic number theorist specializing in automorphic forms and L-functions, focusing on arithmetic statistics, equidistribution of geometric invariants, and the distribution and moments of automorphic periods.

name: Damian Longin Osajda
title: Professor
summary: Damian Osajda is a professor of mathematics at the University of Copenhagen specializing in geometric group theory, particularly groups acting on spaces with nonpositive curvature-like features. His research explores properties of Coxeter groups, systolic groups, CAT(0) complexes, and small cancellation groups.

name: Giovanni Pantuso
title: Associate Professor
summary: Giovanni Pantuso is an operations research mathematician who develops mathematical methods for optimization problems under uncertainty, with emphasis on decision-dependent uncertainty and exact algorithms. His research focuses on practical applications in transportation and logistics domains, including carsharing systems, maritime fleet planning, and ride-sharing networks.

name: Fabien Pazuki
title: Professor
summary: Fabien Pazuki is a professor of mathematics specializing in diophantine geometry, with particular focus on height theory on abelian varieties over number fields and function fields, as well as the arithmetic of rational points, elliptic curves, Drinfeld modules, and arithmetic dynamics.

name: Henrik Laurberg Pedersen
title: Professor
summary: Henrik Laurberg Pedersen is a professor of mathematics at the University of Copenhagen whose research focuses on mathematical analysis with emphasis on special functions, orthogonal polynomials, and complex analysis methods. His work includes studying completely monotonic functions, Bernstein function families, and the dynamical properties of polynomials.

name: Thomas Vils Pedersen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Thomas Vils Pedersen is an Associate Professor of Mathematics at the University of Copenhagen specializing in functional analysis and operator theory. His research focuses on bounded operators in Banach spaces, Banach algebras of functions, and weighted functional spaces.

name: Jesper Lund Pedersen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Jesper Lund Pedersen works on applied probability with focus on financial mathematics, insurance mathematics, and optimal stopping/control problems. His research spans derivative pricing, insurance asset valuation, and real-time signal detection with applications to engineering and natural sciences.

name: Rolf Poulsen
title: Professor
summary: Rolf Poulsen is a Professor of Mathematical Finance at the University of Copenhagen who specializes in quantitative finance, including the development and application of stochastic models for derivatives pricing, volatility dynamics, optimal portfolio choice, and financial planning problems related to mortgages and pensions.

name: Emanuel Reinecke
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Emanuel Reinecke is an arithmetic and algebraic geometer specializing in p-adic geometry. His research focuses on p-adic invariants, moduli spaces, and homotopical methods, using tools like perfectoid spaces and prismatic F-crystals to study geometric objects defined by polynomial equations.

name: Morten S. Risager
title: Professor
summary: Morten S. Risager is a professor of analytic number theory who works on problems in automorphic forms, modular symbols, and hyperbolic geometry, using analytic techniques and L-function theory to study geometric and arithmetic questions on modular surfaces and their generalizations.

name: Mikael Rørdam
title: Professor
summary: Mikael Rørdam is a professor of mathematics at the University of Copenhagen specializing in operator algebras and C*-algebras, which are algebras of operators on Hilbert spaces originally developed as the mathematical framework for quantum mechanics. His research focuses on classification, dimension theory (particularly stable rank), K-theory, and the interplay between groups, dynamics, and operator algebra structure.

name: Henrik Schlichtkrull
title: Professor
summary: Henrik Schlichtkrull is a professor of mathematics specializing in representation theory, harmonic analysis on symmetric spaces, and related areas of abstract algebra. His research focuses on the decomposition of function spaces on symmetric spaces through representation theory, including Plancherel theory for real spherical spaces and the study of discrete series representations.

name: David Glavind Skovmand
title: Associate Professor
summary: David Glavind Skovmand is an Associate Professor of Financial Mathematics at the University of Copenhagen specializing in mathematical models of interest rate dynamics, particularly using Lévy processes for term structure modeling. His research focuses on pricing and hedging interest rate derivatives, the transition from LIBOR to risk-free rates (SOFR), and computational finance methods including Fourier transform approaches.

name: Jan Philip Solovej
title: Professor
summary: Jan Philip Solovej is a mathematical physicist who develops rigorous mathematical foundations for quantum mechanical systems, focusing on the stability and structure of atoms, molecules, and macroscopic matter including Bose gases and superconducting/superfluid states.

name: Pieter Patrick B Spaas
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Pieter Spaas is a mathematician specializing in operator algebras and ergodic theory, with research focused on structural properties and classification problems in von Neumann algebras, particularly Cartan subalgebras, tensor product decompositions, and their connections to group actions and equivalence relations.

name: Mogens Steffensen
title: Head of Department
summary: Mogens Steffensen is a Professor of Life Insurance Mathematics who specializes in actuarial mathematics, optimal control theory, and financial applications to insurance. His research focuses on designing pension products, solving optimal consumption-investment problems, and developing mathematical models for life and health insurance policies using stochastic control methods and portfolio optimization techniques.

name: Daniel Stilck França
title: Associate Professor
summary: Daniel Stilck França is an associate professor at the University of Copenhagen working on mathematical aspects of quantum information and computation. His research focuses on characterizing and quantifying noise in quantum systems, quantum simulators, quantum learning, quantum algorithms for convex optimization, and understanding how noise affects quantum computational capabilities.

name: Helle Sørensen
title: Professor
summary: Helle Sørensen is a Professor of Statistics specializing in functional data analysis, statistical inference for stochastic processes, and biostatistics with extensive applications to biology and related sciences, including agricultural and environmental data.

name: Asger Dag Törnquist
title: Associate Professor
summary: Asger Dag Törnquist works on descriptive set theory and its applications, particularly group actions, orbit equivalence relations, and classification problems in operator algebras and ergodic theory. His recent research focuses on Borel dynamics, distributed algorithms, and connections between dynamical systems and computational theory.

name: Nathalie Wahl
title: Professor
summary: Nathalie Wahl is a mathematician working at the interface of geometric topology, homotopy theory, and algebraic topology, with particular expertise in homological stability, mapping class groups, and the topological properties of manifolds and group automorphisms.

name: Sebastian Weichwald
title: Associate Professor
summary: Sebastian Weichwald is an Associate Professor of Statistics at University of Copenhagen, focusing on causal inference and causal discovery methods. His research bridges causality, statistics, and machine learning, with applications to neuroimaging, medicine, and environmental science.

name: Albert H. Werner
title: Associate Professor
summary: Albert H. Werner is a mathematical physicist who works at the intersection of mathematical physics and quantum information theory. His research focuses on quantum simulation, quantum computing, quantum many-body systems, tensor networks, and developing robust methods for characterizing and calibrating quantum hardware.

name: Johannes Wiesel
title: Associate Professor
summary: Johannes Wiesel is an Associate Professor at University of Copenhagen studying mathematical finance and statistics with focus on optimal transport of stochastic processes and robust model-independent approaches to pricing, hedging, and portfolio optimization.

name: Carsten Wiuf
title: Professor
summary: Carsten Wiuf is a mathematician and statistician who applies probability and statistics to biological sciences, with research spanning stochastic reaction networks, population genetics, coalescent theory, and the development of mathematical tools to infer human population history and evolutionary patterns from genetic data.

name: Jun Yang
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Jun Yang is a statistician working on computational statistics and Bayesian learning theory, with primary interests in high-dimensional Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithms, sampling from heavy-tailed distributions, and statistical inference for nonlinear/non-stationary time series.

name: Anton Rask Lundborg
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Anton Rask Lundborg develops flexible statistical methods with applications to causal inference, using nonparametric and semiparametric techniques. His research focuses on variable significance testing, conditional independence testing, and inference with specialized data types including functional data, compositional data, and multimodal data.

name: Markus Tobias Ahlers
title: Associate Professor
summary: Markus Ahlers studies high-energy astroparticle physics, focusing on cosmic rays, gamma rays, and neutrinos through multi-messenger observations. His research centers on understanding cosmic ray transport phenomena and utilizing neutrino telescopes like IceCube to probe astrophysical sources and physics beyond the Standard Model.

name: Anja C. Andersen
title: Professor
summary: Anja C. Andersen is an astrophysicist whose research focuses primarily on cosmic dust in stellar envelopes and interstellar clouds, including dust properties, composition, and their role in galaxy evolution and planet formation. She also holds the position of Professor for the Public Understanding of Science and Technology at the University of Copenhagen.

name: Brian Møller Andersen
title: Professor
summary: Brian Møller Andersen is a theoretical condensed matter physicist who specializes in the study of unconventional superconductors, particularly high-temperature superconductors like cuprates and iron-based superconductors, focusing on the interplay between magnetic order, impurities, and superconducting states.

name: Alexander Baklanov
title: Professor
summary: Alexander Baklanov is a Professor at the Niels Bohr Institute specializing in integrated modeling for meteorological and chemical weather prediction, with particular focus on urban-scale atmospheric processes, air pollution dynamics, and environmental impact assessment in megacities.

name: Ian Bearden
title: Professor
summary: Ian Bearden is a Professor of Physics at the Niels Bohr Institute who studies quark-gluon plasma and ultrarelativistic heavy ion reactions, with extensive publications from the ALICE collaboration at CERN. His research focuses on experimental particle physics methods and detector development for high-energy nuclear experiments.

name: Ivanka M Orozova Bekkevold
title: Associate Professor
summary: Ivanka Orozova Bekkevold studies crustal geodynamics and stress-strain evolution in sedimentary basins, with applications to geothermal energy exploration, CO2 storage in hydrocarbon reservoirs, and earthquake hazard assessment. Her research combines numerical forward modeling, geophysical data integration, and basin analysis to understand subsurface processes.

name: Pól Martin Bendix
title: Associate Professor
summary: Pól Martin Bendix is an associate professor of biophysics at the Niels Bohr Institute whose research focuses on understanding the physical principles governing cell surface structures and dynamics. His work combines optical manipulation techniques and plasmonic heating of nanostructures to study cellular phenomena including membrane repair, curvature-induced protein sorting, and viral-relevant membrane processes.

name: Emil Bjerrum-Bohr
title: Associate Professor
summary: Emil Bjerrum-Bohr is an associate professor specializing in amplitude computations for gauge theories, quantum gravity, and quantum chromodynamics. His research uses advanced methods including on-shell amplitudes, worldline effective field theory, and twistor-inspired approaches to understand particle interactions and gravitational wave physics from black hole collisions.

name: Thomas Blunier
title: Professor
summary: Thomas Blunier is a professor at the Niels Bohr Institute studying paleoclimate through ice core analysis, particularly from Greenland and Antarctica. His research focuses on reconstructing past atmospheric greenhouse gas concentrations (methane, CO2, nitrous oxide) and climate variability spanning thousands to hundreds of thousands of years.

name: Gabriel Brammer
title: Associate Professor
summary: Gabriel Brammer studies galaxy formation and evolution across cosmic time using deep imaging and spectroscopic surveys, from massive nearby galaxies to infant galaxies in the early universe, primarily leveraging Hubble Space Telescope data and developing new approaches with the James Webb Space Telescope.

name: Tulio Brito Brasil
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Tulio Brito Brasil works on quantum optics and photonics, focusing on generating and characterizing entangled photonic states for quantum sensing and metrology applications. His research includes demonstrating quantum behavior in atomic spin oscillators and developing high-purity Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen states for enhanced precision measurements.

name: Mauricio Bustamante
title: Associate Professor
summary: Mauricio Bustamante is a theoretical astroparticle physicist working on high-energy astrophysical neutrinos discovered by IceCube, focusing on their sources, spectra, flavor composition, and connections to ultra-high-energy cosmic rays. His research explores fundamental physics beyond the Standard Model, including tests of Lorentz invariance, neutrino decay, and new particle interactions using observations from neutrino telescopes.

name: Jean-Baptiste Sylvain Béguin
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Jean-Baptiste Béguin develops quantum optical systems integrating photonic nanophotonics with ultracold atoms in optical tweezers, exploring quantum nondemolition measurements and light-atom interactions for quantum information science and many-body physics.

name: Alessandra Camplani
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Alessandra Camplani works on experimental particle physics detector upgrades, specifically developing FPGA firmware and electronics for the ATLAS experiment at CERN. Her research focuses on trigger systems, particle tracking algorithms, and real-time data acquisition hardware for high-luminosity LHC detector upgrades.

name: Vitor Cardoso
title: Professor
summary: Vitor Cardoso is a theoretical physicist specializing in strong gravity, black holes, and gravitational waves. He uses black holes as engines of discovery to test Einstein's General Relativity, probe dark matter, and advance gravitational wave astronomy through observation and theory.

name: Andrew Alan Chael
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Andrew Chael is a theoretical astrophysicist studying supermassive black holes and their extreme environments through general relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) simulations and computational imaging. His research focuses on understanding how black holes power relativistic jets and uses data from the Event Horizon Telescope to directly image black hole shadows, particularly in Sgr A* and M87.

name: Gang Chen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Gang Chen is an Assistant Professor at the Niels Bohr Institute specializing in theoretical high energy physics and gravitational physics. His research focuses on scattering amplitude calculations, double copy constructions, and their applications to gravitational wave and black hole physics.

name: Lise Bech Christensen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Lise Bech Christensen studies galaxy formation and evolution at high redshifts using strong gravitational lensing as cosmic telescopes to magnify faint distant galaxies. Her research focuses on understanding how galaxies accumulate and recycle gas, metal enrichment processes, and the properties of damped Lyman-alpha absorbers and gamma-ray burst host galaxies.

name: Jens Hesselbjerg Christensen
title: Professor
summary: Jens Hesselbjerg Christensen is a Professor of Climate Physics at the University of Copenhagen who specializes in regional climate modeling, particularly the development and application of the HIRHAM regional climate model. His research focuses on climate change impacts at regional scales, including extreme weather events, Arctic climate phenomena, and high-resolution climate projections for Europe and Greenland.

name: Morten Holm Christensen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Morten Holm Christensen is a theoretical condensed matter physicist who studies exotic superconducting and magnetic states, particularly in kagome systems and frustrated lattices, where superconductivity and magnetism interact in unusual ways.

name: Eliza Cook
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Eliza Cook is an ice core scientist who uses tephra (volcanic ash) layers and other proxies preserved in Greenland ice cores to reconstruct past volcanism and climate change. Her research focuses on developing dating methods and environmental reconstruction techniques for Arctic ice cores and studying volcanic eruptions recorded in ice records.

name: Dorthe Dahl-Jensen
title: Professor
summary: Dorthe Dahl-Jensen is a paleoclimatologist who leads ice core research at the Niels Bohr Institute, studying deep ice cores from Greenland to reconstruct past climate and understand ice sheet behavior. Her work integrates ice core analysis with climate modeling to assess how the Greenland ice sheet responds to climate change and to project future sea level rise.

name: Mogens Dam
title: Professor
summary: Mogens Dam is a professor of experimental particle physics at the Niels Bohr Institute who conducts research with the ATLAS detector at the Large Hadron Collider, focusing on precision measurements of particle interactions and detector development for future circular colliders.

name: Poul Henrik Damgaard
title: Professor
summary: Poul Henrik Damgaard is a theoretical physicist who develops modern amplitude computation techniques to solve problems in gravitational physics and quantum field theory. His current research focuses on applying scattering amplitude methods to compute gravitational wave signals and classical two-body dynamics in general relativity, as well as non-perturbative lattice studies of highly supersymmetric theories.

name: Peter Ditlevsen
title: Professor
summary: Peter Ditlevsen is a professor of climate physics who researches climate tipping points, abrupt transitions in the climate system, and nonlinear dynamics. His work combines mathematical approaches from complex systems theory, turbulence, and statistical physics to understand and predict catastrophic climate shifts like the collapse of ocean circulation or ice sheet instability.

name: Amin Doostmohammadi
title: Associate Professor
summary: Amin Doostmohammadi is an associate professor studying how physical forces and topological defects drive biological organization, particularly in collective cell dynamics, tissue development, and active matter systems. His research bridges physics and biology to understand emergent patterns and self-organization in living systems ranging from bacterial suspensions to developing tissues.

name: Jose Maria Ezquiaga
title: Associate Professor
summary: Jose Maria Ezquiaga uses gravitational wave observations from LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA to probe fundamental physics, dark energy, and modified gravity theories. His research spans cosmological tests using binary black hole mergers, gravitational wave lensing searches, and constraints on the nature of dark matter and dark energy.

name: Karsten Flensberg
title: Professor
summary: Karsten Flensberg is director of the Center of Quantum Devices and focuses on quantum effects in electron transport in condensed matter devices and quantum information systems. His recent research emphasizes topological superconductors, superconductor-semiconductor hybrid platforms, and Majorana bound states as platforms for topological quantum computation.

name: Johan Peter Uldall Fynbo
title: Professor
summary: Fynbo studies galaxies primarily in the young universe, with a particular focus on using gamma-ray bursts as probes of early cosmic star formation and chemical evolution. His research involves optical spectroscopy of GRB afterglows to measure redshifts and characterize host galaxies and absorbing systems along the line of sight.

name: Vasileios Gkinis
title: Associate Professor
summary: Vasileios Gkinis is an Associate Professor at the Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth group (NBI) specializing in paleoclimate reconstruction using ice core records, particularly from Greenland, employing water isotope geochemistry and laser spectroscopy methods to understand past climate changes and abrupt climate transitions.

name: Aslak Grinsted
title: Associate Professor
summary: Aslak Grinsted is an Associate Professor at the Niels Bohr Institute studying ice dynamics, climate variability, and sea level change using mathematical modelling, data analysis, and satellite observations. His research focuses on understanding ice sheet behavior, reconstructing past climate from ice cores, and projecting future sea level rise under different climate scenarios.

name: Steen Harle Hansen
title: Professor
summary: Steen Harle Hansen studies dark matter particle properties and the characteristics of dark matter structures, particularly measuring density and velocity anisotropy profiles in equilibrated systems like galaxy clusters. He also investigates the origin and properties of hot gas in these structures, including temperature and density profiles.

name: Jørgen Beck Hansen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Jørgen Beck Hansen conducts experimental particle physics research focused on the ATLAS detector at the LHC, studying electromagnetic calorimeter performance, quarkonium production mechanisms, and gauge boson couplings in proton-proton and heavy ion collisions.

name: Troels Harmark
title: Associate Professor
summary: Troels Harmark is a theoretical physicist specializing in string theory, general relativity, and the AdS/CFT correspondence. His research focuses on non-relativistic limits of gauge theories and string theories, black hole dynamics, integrability methods, and Spin Matrix theories that emerge near BPS bounds.

name: Troels Haugbølle
title: Associate Professor
summary: Troels Haugbølle is a computational astrophysicist who develops numerical simulations and models of star and planet formation, focusing on early stages of stellar evolution including protostellar collapse, disk formation, and astrochemical processes.

name: Per Hedegård
title: Professor
summary: Per Hedegård is a condensed matter physicist who applies quantum mechanics to describe solid matter and molecules. His research spans electron transport through nanoscale systems and single molecules, superconductivity, spintronics, and emerging areas like phononics and metal-organic materials.

name: Thomas Rainer Heimburg
title: Professor
summary: Thomas Heimburg is a professor of biophysics at the Niels Bohr Institute who leads the Membrane Biophysics Group, conducting theoretical and experimental research on thermodynamics and spectroscopy of biological and artificial membranes. He is notably known for proposing that nerve pulses propagate as electromechanical solitons (sound waves) through membranes, challenging the classical Hodgkin-Huxley model of electrical signaling.

name: Asta Heinesen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Asta Heinesen works on theoretical and observational cosmology, developing model-independent methods to analyze cosmic expansion and probe dark energy. Her research focuses on cosmography, redshift drift, and gravitational wave events as tools to understand the structure and dynamics of the Universe without assuming specific cosmological models.

name: Kasper Elm Heintz
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Kasper Elm Heintz is an assistant professor studying astrophysical transients as tools to probe galaxy evolution across cosmic time. His research focuses on observing fast radio bursts, gamma-ray bursts, kilonovae, and quasars to understand the properties and evolution of galaxies in the early universe, particularly through neutral gas and chemical abundance measurements.

name: Jens Hjorth
title: Professor
summary: Jens Hjorth is a professor of astrophysics at the Niels Bohr Institute who directs the Dark Cosmology Centre, focusing on cosmological questions through the study of transient phenomena including gamma-ray bursts, supernovae, and gravitational waves to understand dark matter, dark energy, cosmic dust, and the origin of heavy elements in the universe.

name: Christine Schøtt Hvidberg
title: Professor
summary: Christine Schøtt Hvidberg is a Professor of Climate Physics who studies ice sheet dynamics, mass balance, and flow using numerical modeling, field observations, and ice core data. Her research focuses on how Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets respond to climate change and contribute to sea level rise, with additional work on Martian polar ice.

name: Mogens Høgh Jensen
title: Professor
summary: Mogens Høgh Jensen is a professor of complex systems and biophysics who studies the dynamics of biological systems, particularly focusing on oscillations in p53 and gene expression networks. His research bridges classical complex systems theory (chaos, fractals, multifractals, turbulence) with modern biophysics, examining how dynamical principles govern cellular signaling and DNA repair processes.

name: Markus Jochum
title: Professor
summary: Markus Jochum is a Professor of Physical Oceanography at the Niels Bohr Institute who uses numerical ocean models to investigate ocean dynamics, climate systems, and paleoclimate events. His research focuses on ocean circulation, vertical mixing parameterization, and testing hypotheses about past climate variability.

name: Jes Kristian Jørgensen
title: Professor
summary: Jørgensen studies the formation and early evolution of young protostars and their protoplanetary disks using ALMA and other submillimeter telescopes, focusing on understanding the physical and chemical properties that determine planetary system outcomes. His research spans molecular spectroscopy, ice inventories, complex organic molecules, and the interconnected physics and chemistry during star and planet formation.

name: Uffe Gråe Jørgensen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Uffe Gråe Jørgensen is an astrophysicist studying exoplanets and planetary systems, with particular expertise in microlensing techniques for planet detection, stellar physics, and the potential for habitable worlds. His research combines observational astronomy with modeling of stellar and planetary environments to understand the prevalence and characteristics of planetary systems across the galaxy.

name: Eigil Kaas
title: Professor
summary: Eigil Kaas is a climate scientist specializing in climate modeling, numerical weather prediction, and atmospheric dynamics. His research focuses on numerical methods in atmospheric models, climate dynamics, extreme weather phenomena, and machine learning applications to weather prediction and atmospheric parameterization.

name: Koki Kakiichi
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Koki Kakiichi is an astrophysicist who studies the formation and evolution of the first galaxies, supermassive black holes, and cosmic reionization during the first billion years of the Universe, combining theoretical work with observational analysis using tools like the James Webb Space Telescope.

name: Yinhui Kan
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Yinhui Kan is an Assistant Professor whose research focuses on nanophotonics and quantum emitters, particularly exploring light-matter interactions at the nanoscale through systems like nanodiamonds and engineered quantum emitters for photonic applications.

name: Kunihiko Kaneko
title: Professor
summary: Kunihiko Kaneko investigates universal principles underlying life systems by applying theoretical physics and dynamical systems approaches to understand adaptation, evolution, and development across hierarchical biological levels from molecules to ecosystems. His work emphasizes macro-micro consistency as a guiding principle for discovering general laws in cellular systems, protocell origins, and evolutionary dynamics.

name: Morten Kjaergaard
title: Associate Professor
summary: Morten Kjaergaard is an Associate Professor working on superconducting quantum information physics, focusing on building quantum computers and understanding their behavior through qubit characterization, error correction schemes, and advanced measurement techniques for detecting rapid fluctuations in quantum processors.

name: Helle Astrid Kjær
title: Associate Professor
summary: Helle Astrid Kjær develops methods for continuous chemical detection in ice cores to understand past climate and environmental conditions. Her research focuses on analyzing Greenland ice cores and Holocene sea ice, investigating chemical composition including nutrients like phosphorus and halogens (iodine, bromine) to reconstruct paleoclimate records.

name: Charlotte Fløe Kristjansen
title: Professor
summary: Charlotte Fløe Kristjansen is a theoretical physicist specializing in exact solutions to quantum field theory and string theory problems. Her research focuses on integrability, defect conformal field theories with holographic duals, spin chains, and quantum quenches, with applications spanning high energy physics and condensed matter.

name: D. Jason Koskinen
title: Associate Professor
summary: D. Jason Koskinen is an Associate Professor at the Niels Bohr Institute who leads IceCube neutrino observatory research, focusing on precision measurements of neutrino oscillations and searches for beyond-Standard-Model physics using high-energy astrophysical and atmospheric neutrino detection.

name: Andreas Haahr Larsen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Andreas Haahr Larsen applies molecular modelling and experimental techniques including X-ray and neutron scattering (SAXS/SANS) to unravel structural and dynamical aspects of membrane protein-lipid interactions. His research includes studies of C2 domains, AMPA-type glutamate receptors, nanodisc development, and GPCR trafficking.

name: William Iain Leonard Lawrie
title: Assistant Professor
summary: William Lawrie is a quantum computing and solid-state physics researcher who specializes in developing quantum dot qubits in semiconductor systems, particularly using germanium heterostructures proximitized by superconductors for topological quantum computing applications.

name: Kim Lefmann
title: Professor
summary: Kim Lefmann is a condensed matter physicist specializing in quantum magnetism, frustrated magnetic systems, and superconductivity, studied primarily through neutron scattering techniques. His research includes developing neutron scattering instrumentation and has recently confirmed theoretical predictions about domain wall oscillations in magnetic materials.

name: Shikai Liu
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Shikai Liu is an Assistant Professor working on quantum optics and nonlinear optics, with focus on generating high-fidelity entangled states through light-matter interactions in solid-state systems for quantum technologies.

name: Peter Lodahl
title: Professor
summary: Peter Lodahl develops quantum hardware based on quantum dots coupled to photonic nanostructures for quantum information processing. His group creates deterministic single-photon sources, spin-photon interfaces, and photonic quantum gates for applications in quantum communication, quantum simulation, and quantum computing.

name: Johannes Jakob Lohmann
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Johannes Jakob Lohmann studies climate tipping points and abrupt climate transitions, focusing on both rate-induced and bifurcation-induced tipping mechanisms in complex systems like ocean circulation, using dynamical systems theory, climate models, and paleoclimate ice core data to understand critical transitions and develop early-warning indicators.

name: Andres Luna Godoy
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Andres Luna Godoy works at the interface between gravitation and scattering amplitudes in quantum field theories, focusing on generalizing the double copy to classical gravitational systems and applying scattering amplitude techniques to gravitational-wave physics and binary dynamics.

name: Morten Bo Madsen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Morten Bo Madsen is an astrophysicist and planetary scientist who has led the Mars Group at Copenhagen's Niels Bohr Institute since 2002, specializing in analyzing the mineralogy and magnetic properties of Martian surface materials. His research has contributed to NASA Mars missions from Pathfinder through Perseverance, using spectroscopy and instrumentation to understand Mars' geological history, water ice presence, and habitability.

name: Charles M. Marcus
title: Professor
summary: Charles Marcus is an experimental condensed matter physicist whose research focuses on quantum devices, topological states of matter, and quantum computing. His work centers on developing hybrid semiconductor-superconductor systems for quantum information applications and exploring measurement-induced quantum phenomena in synthetic quantum matter.

name: Charlotte Mason
title: Associate Professor
summary: Charlotte Mason is an astrophysicist studying the formation and evolution of the first galaxies in the early universe and how they caused the reionization of hydrogen. Her work combines theoretical models and observations to understand the cosmic dawn epoch.

name: Conor John Ryan Mc Partland
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Conor McPartland studies how active galactic nuclei influence star formation and morphological transformation in galaxies across cosmic time, using optical/near-infrared spectroscopy and galaxy morphology analysis. His research investigates the mechanisms driving galaxy quenching and the evolution of galaxy structure from the early universe to the present day.

name: Leonardo Midolo
title: Associate Professor
summary: Leonardo Midolo develops quantum photonic technologies based on single-photon sources from semiconductor quantum dots integrated with nanophotonic circuits. His research focuses on scalable photonic platforms for quantum communication and quantum computing applications.

name: Nicola Minafra
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Nicola Minafra is an experimental particle physicist specializing in detector development and instrumentation for high-energy physics experiments. His research focuses on fast timing detectors, advanced sensor technologies (including CVD diamonds and silicon detectors), and detector prototyping for LHC experiments, particularly the ALICE FoCal calorimeter upgrade.

name: Namiko Mitarai
title: Associate Professor
summary: Namiko Mitarai is a biophysicist applying statistical physics methods to understand bacterial systems and phage-bacteria interactions. Her research focuses on quantitative rules governing viral growth, bacterial survival strategies under stress, and gene expression dynamics using both theoretical modeling and experimental work.

name: Amalie Sofie Jensen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Jensen studies how individuals respond economically and politically to government policies, focusing on public economics and political economy. Her research examines wealth inequality's impact on welfare preferences, household economic behavior, and voter attitudes toward economic policies and redistribution.

name: Anders Munk-Nielsen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Anders Munk-Nielsen is an associate professor working in empirical industrial organization, applying structural econometrics and computational methods to analyze consumer behavior and market competition in industries like automobiles, pharmaceuticals, and housing.

name: Anders Rahbek
title: Professor
summary: Anders Rahbek is a Professor of Econometrics at University of Copenhagen who specializes in developing econometric and statistical methods for analyzing financial and macroeconomic time series, with particular expertise in GARCH models, bootstrap inference, cointegration testing, and advanced time series analysis including duration models and nonlinear dynamics.

name: Andreas Bjerre-Nielsen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Andreas Bjerre-Nielsen is an Associate Professor in Economics and Social Data Science at University of Copenhagen who studies how digital technologies and education policies affect human behavior and student outcomes. His research combines economics and data science methods to examine intelligent learning systems, school choice policies, and the role of social networks in academic performance.

name: Anne Sofie Beck Knudsen
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Anne Sofie Beck Knudsen studies how cultural norms and institutions shape economic development and long-run outcomes, with particular focus on historical migration patterns, religious diversity, and the evolution of individualistic versus collectivist values in Northwestern Europe.

name: Asger Lau Andersen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Asger Lau Andersen studies household economic decisions, particularly how households respond to wealth shocks, manage debt and consumption, and are affected by monetary policy. He also conducts research in political economics using administrative microdata and applied econometric methods.

name: Asger Mose Wingender
title: Associate Professor
summary: Asger Mose Wingender is an associate professor at University of Copenhagen who studies economic growth, agricultural development, and the origins of democracy. His research examines how agricultural innovations, irrigation systems, and structural economic transformation shape long-term political and economic outcomes across countries.

name: Bertel Schjerning
title: Professor
summary: Bertel Schjerning is a specialist in structural microeconometrics and numerical dynamic programming who develops computational methods to solve and estimate models of intertemporal economic behavior. His research focuses on dynamic games with multiple equilibria, dynamic discrete choice models, entrepreneurial behavior, automobile markets, and strategic firm investment dynamics.

name: Björn Thor Arnarson
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Arnarson studies international trade and labor economics, focusing on how exporting firms behave, how trade shocks propagate through supply chains and service sectors, and the labor market effects of cross-border commuting and worker mobility.

name: Brigitte Hochmuth
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Brigitte Hochmuth is a quantitative macroeconomist who uses heterogeneous-agent models and microdata to study how policy shocks and labor market reforms shape aggregate and distributional outcomes, with emphasis on household heterogeneity and the redistributive effects of fiscal and labor market policies.

name: Carl-Johan Lars Dalgaard
title: Professor
summary: Carl-Johan Dalgaard is a Professor of Economics at University of Copenhagen whose research focuses on economic growth and development, examining how factors like migration, climate, health, and technological diffusion shape long-term productivity and income differences across nations.

name: Casper Worm Hansen
title: Professor
summary: Casper Worm Hansen is a professor of economics at the University of Copenhagen who studies the determinants of the demographic transition in developed countries and how these fundamental population changes influenced economic and social development. His research spans economic history, macroeconomics of development, and population health, with publications examining life expectancy, mortality, immigration, agricultural innovation, and long-run economic growth.

name: Christian Skov Jensen
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Christian Skov Jensen is an Assistant Professor of Financial Economics at University of Copenhagen with main research interests in asset pricing, financial economics, macro-finance, financial econometrics, and derivatives. His research examines time variation in stock return distributions, finding that returns are more left skewed and fat tailed during good times than bad times, creating pro-cyclical variation in conditional tail risk.

name: Christina Gravert
title: Associate Professor
summary: Christina Gravert specializes in behavioral economics with a focus on environmental decision-making and policy design, examining behavioral barriers to sustainable choices such as energy-efficient technology adoption and food waste behavior. She uses large-scale field experiments, surveys, and administrative data to inform evidence-based climate policy.

name: Claus Thustrup Kreiner
title: Professor
summary: Claus Thustrup Kreiner is a professor of public economics who studies how taxation, welfare policy, and social behavior shape inequality in income, wealth, and health. His research examines behavioral responses to public policy using both empirical methods and theoretical analysis, with a focus on designing optimal tax and transfer systems.

name: Daniel le Maire
title: Associate Professor
summary: Daniel le Maire specializes in labor economics, search theory, and applied microeconometrics. His recent work examines manager-specific wage premiums and selection in managerial labor markets, alongside studies of household liquidity constraints and labor market outcomes using natural experiments from Danish mortgage reforms.

name: Daphné Jocelyne Skandalis
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Daphné Skandalis is a labor economist who studies how unemployment insurance, job search decisions, and labor market policies affect worker behavior and inequality, using applied econometrics methods including online job application data and regression discontinuity designs.

name: Edward Samuel Jones
title: Associate Professor
summary: Edward Samuel Jones is a development economist specializing in applied economic and policy analysis in Sub-Saharan Africa, with research focusing on foreign aid effectiveness, education, labor markets, economic growth, and poverty measurement. He combines macro- and micro-economic data with empirical methods and has over a decade of field experience advising African governments.

name: Egor Starkov
title: Associate Professor
summary: Egor Starkov specializes in microeconomic theory with a focus on dynamic models of information transmission, including strategic communication through signaling, disclosure, and cheap talk. His recent work explores behavioral economics topics such as consumer learning, targeted advertising, social learning design, and rational inattention in complex environments.

name: Finn Tarp
title: Professor
summary: Finn Tarp is a Professor of Development Economics at the University of Copenhagen with over 45 years of experience researching development strategy, foreign aid, poverty reduction, and income distribution primarily in African and Asian developing countries. His research employs both theoretical and empirical methods to analyze macroeconomic policy, agricultural sector development, and the effectiveness of development interventions.

name: Florian Hans Fred Schneider
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Florian Schneider is a behavioral economist who combines laboratory experiments, surveys, and administrative data to study how moral beliefs, social preferences, and prosocial motivations affect labor markets and health behavior choices. His research examines wage premiums in morally controversial work, the effectiveness of financial incentives for health behaviors like vaccination, and how individual moral values influence economic decisions.

name: Franziska Valder
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Franziska Valder is an applied microeconomist and health economist who studies the effects of health policy interventions (particularly hormonal contraception and mental health care payment systems) on individual outcomes including mental health, labor market performance, and wage-setting behavior.

name: Frikk Nesje
title: Associate Professor
summary: Frikk Nesje is an environmental and resource economist who researches how to value long-term public investments, particularly in climate and sustainability. His work focuses on social discount rates, intergenerational equity, carbon pricing policies, and decision-making under risk of ecological tipping points.

name: Hannes Ullrich
title: Associate Professor
summary: Applied microeconomist studying empirical industrial organization, health economics, and digitization. His research spans antibiotic prescribing patterns and resistance, data economics in digital platforms, and labor incentives using techniques like machine learning and discrete choice models.

name: Hans Jørgen Whitta-Jacobsen
title: Professor
summary: Hans Jørgen Whitta-Jacobsen is a Professor of Economics at the University of Copenhagen who specializes in macroeconomic theory, focusing on growth models, income distribution, business cycles, and the long-run effects of economic policy. His research also extends to environmental economics and game-theoretic approaches to economic behavior.

name: Heino Bohn Nielsen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Heino Bohn Nielsen is a Professor of Economics and Econometrics specializing in econometric methods for financial and macroeconomic time series data. His research focuses on developing bootstrap methods for time series econometrics, cointegration analysis, volatility modeling, and advancing statistical techniques for testing and forecasting in nonlinear autoregressive models.

name: Henrik Hansen
title: Head of Department
summary: Henrik Hansen is a development economist focused on applied econometric analysis of firms and households in the Global South, with particular expertise in foreign aid macroeconomic impacts, labor market outcomes, firm productivity, and impact evaluation methodologies using survey data.

name: Jakob Egholt Søgaard
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Jakob Egholt Søgaard is an assistant professor at the Center for Economic Behavior and Inequality (CEBI) at University of Copenhagen who specializes in public and labor economics, focusing on how taxation, benefit systems, and family policies affect labor supply decisions, gender inequality, and income inequality.

name: Jakob Roland Munch
title: Professor
summary: Jakob Roland Munch is a professor of economics specializing in labor and international trade issues, particularly how globalization, offshoring, automation, and international trade affect worker wages, employment, and labor market outcomes. His research uses matched worker-firm data to examine the impacts of trade flows, outsourcing, and firm internationalization on labor markets and environmental outcomes.

name: Jeanet Sinding Bentzen
title: Professor
summary: Jeanet Sinding Bentzen is an economist studying the causes and consequences of religion for economic development and societal outcomes. Her research uses large empirical datasets spanning multiple countries and millions of individuals to analyze how religiosity influences economic growth, institutions, cultural values, and behavior patterns across societies.

name: Jeppe Druedahl
title: Associate Professor
summary: Jeppe Druedahl is a macroeconomist who researches consumption-saving decisions, economic inequality, and crises using heterogeneous agent models and computational methods. His work emphasizes numerical solution techniques, applications of deep learning to economic models, and empirical evidence from administrative data on household financial behavior.

name: Jesper Eriksen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Jesper Eriksen studies the determinants of educational and labor market opportunities, examining how large-scale educational reforms affect social mobility across generations and how peers influence educational choices. He uses quasi-experimental econometric methods with Danish administrative data to analyze education and labor market outcomes, with particular focus on vocational education, school choice, and disadvantaged populations.

name: Jesper Riis-Vestergaard Sørensen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Jesper Riis-Vestergaard Sørensen is an econometrician specializing in high-dimensional estimation methods, discrete choice modeling, and nonparametric econometric techniques for cross-sectional and panel data. His work focuses on developing theoretical and computational methods for modern statistical estimation in economics.

name: Johan Lagerlöf
title: Associate Professor
summary: Johan Lagerlöf is an applied microeconomist specializing in game theory and contract theory applications to industrial organization and political economy. His research examines how incomplete information affects strategic behavior in markets, contests, insurance, mergers, and electoral competition.

name: John Rand
title: Professor
summary: John Rand is a professor of development economics specializing in firm-level analysis, industrial policy, and impact evaluation of development projects in emerging economies. His research focuses on enterprise dynamics, productivity, governance, and structural change in Asia and Africa, with particular expertise in Vietnam, Myanmar, and southern Africa.

name: John Vincent Kramer
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: John Vincent Kramer is a macroeconomist studying distributional effects of economic shocks using heterogeneous agent models and high-frequency administrative data. His research focuses on how monetary policy, energy shocks, and labor market conditions differentially affect workers across the income distribution.

name: Linea Hasager
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Linea Hasager is an Assistant Professor in labor economics at the University of Copenhagen who specializes in migration economics and refugee integration. Her research uses quasi-experimental methods and administrative data to evaluate integration policies, including language training programs, neighborhood effects on health outcomes, and labor market impacts for refugees and immigrants.

name: Magnus Tolum Buus
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Magnus Tolum Buus is an economist specializing in international trade who studies how firms expand in export markets, with particular focus on network effects and the impact of export support policies using Danish firm-level trade data and causal identification methods.

name: Manuel Menkhoff
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Manuel Menkhoff studies how firms make investment and pricing decisions in response to macroeconomic shocks, monetary policy changes, and tax policies. His research focuses on understanding firm expectations, heterogeneous firm behavior, and information frictions that affect business decision-making during normal and crisis periods.

name: Marc Patrick Brag Klemp
title: Associate Professor
summary: Klemp is an economic historian and development economist who studies deep causes of economic growth through the lens of prehistoric human migration, population diversity, and demographic dynamics. His research uses historical datasets and genealogies to explore how ancient migration patterns, fertility decisions, and cultural diversity shape modern economic outcomes including inequality, conflict, and institutional development.

name: Martin Browning
title: Professor
summary: Martin Browning is an applied microeconometrician who studies household economic behavior, particularly intra-household decision-making between spouses, consumption and time allocation, and the collective model of household choices. His research uses structural econometric methods to test theories of how household members bargain over consumption, work, and leisure decisions.

name: Mette Foged
title: Associate Professor
summary: Mette Foged is an applied labor economist who studies refugee and immigrant integration, focusing on how language training, labor market policies, and institutions affect employment, wages, and economic outcomes. Her research uses econometric methods to evaluate policy reforms and analyze the broader labor market impacts of immigration.

name: Mette Rasmussen
title: Assistant Professor
summary: Mette Rasmussen is a labor economist who studies worker transitions in the labor market and evaluates the effectiveness of active labor market policies using administrative data and causal inference methods, particularly instrumental variable designs based on quasi-random caseworker assignment.

name: Mette Gørtz
title: Professor
summary: Mette Gørtz is a Professor of Economics at University of Copenhagen who studies how family economics, labor markets, and health decisions shape individual outcomes across generations. Her research examines inequality in education and health through the lens of parental investments, personality traits, and policy interventions such as daycare enrollment and social services.

name: Mette Ejrnæs
title: Professor
summary: Mette Ejrnæs is an applied microeconomist specializing in labor economics, family economics, and consumption patterns using panel data methods. Her research examines income and earnings processes, wage dynamics around family events like childbirth, fertility decisions, and the effects of social insurance policies on individual behavior.

name: Michael Bergman
title: Associate Professor
summary: Michael Bergman is an Associate Professor of Economics at the University of Copenhagen specializing in macroeconomic policy analysis, particularly European and Nordic fiscal and monetary frameworks. His research focuses on exchange rates, business cycles, fiscal rules effectiveness, and the relationship between policy uncertainty and economic outcomes.

name: Miriam Wüst
title: Associate Professor
summary: Miriam Wüst is an applied microeconomist who studies health and family economics, with a particular focus on how early-life interventions and health shocks affect children's and families' well-being through long-term outcomes. Her research uses administrative and quasi-experimental methods, often leveraging natural experiments from Danish policy reforms and historical health events.

name: Mogens Fosgerau
title: Professor
summary: Mogens Fosgerau is a professor of economics specializing in discrete choice theory, transportation economics, and behavioral economics. His research focuses on understanding how individuals make decisions about travel, the economic impacts of traffic congestion, and the application of entropy models to spatial choice problems.

name: Morten Bennedsen
title: Professor
summary: Morten Bennedsen's main research area is the governance of family firms and other closely held corporations in a global context. He has done research on family firms, closely held corporations, capital structures, venture capital, investor protection, ownership structures, and privatisation.

name: Morten Graugaard Olsen
title: Associate Professor
summary: Morten Graugaard Olsen studies how technological change drives economic outcomes, particularly using patent text analysis to quantify automation innovations and examining their effects on income inequality, wages, and economic growth. His research integrates labor economics and environmental economics to understand how the direction of innovation responds to economic incentives and market forces.

name: N. Meltem Daysal
title: Associate Professor
summary: Applied microeconomist studying the intersection of health and labor economics. Her research examines health shocks' effects on families, medical interventions' labor market impacts, and housing wealth transmission across generations using register data and natural experiments.

name: Neda Trifkovic
title: Tenure Track Assistant Professor
summary: Neda Trifkovic's research sits at the intersection of development economics, agricultural economics, and food policy, with emphasis on how international institutions and regulations shape economic outcomes for smallholders and firms in developing countries, particularly regarding food standards, value chain participation, and market access in Vietnam, Myanmar, and other regions.

name: Nick Vikander
title: Associate Professor
summary: Nick Vikander is an Associate Professor of Economics at the University of Copenhagen who studies industrial organization, organizational economics, and behavioral economics. His research focuses on how firms design information, communicate with consumers, structure contracts, and influence consumer beliefs and behavior through strategic actions.

name: Niels Johannesen
title: Professor
